date — Date manipulation¶

New in version 0.1.

Module author: Claude Gibert <claude.gibert@synopticview.co.uk>

The Date class¶

class semperpy.core.date.Date(oject)¶

The Date class encapsulates the standard Python datetime object but offers some easier way to manipulate dates. A date is created with an implicit “accuracy”, e.g. its smaller representation: Date handles year, month, day, hour, minute and seconds, and basic operations:

from semperpy.core.date import *

d = Date(2010)
print d.datetime()
2010-01-01 00:00:00
print d + 1, d - 1
2011, 2009

d = Date(201004)
print d.datetime()
2010-04-01 00:00:00
print d + 1, d - 1
2010-05, 2010-03

d = Date(20100401)
print d.datetime()
2010-04-01 00:00:00
print d + 1, d - 1
2010-04-02 2010-03-31

d = Date(2010040112)
print d.datetime()
2010-04-01 12:00:00
print d + 1, d - 1
2010-04-01 13 2010-04-01 11

d = Date(201004011200)
print d.datetime()
2010-04-01 12:00:00
print d + 1, d - 1
2010-04-01 12:01 2010-04-01 11:59

d = Date(20100401120030)
print d.datetime()
2010-04-01 12:00:30
print d + 1, d - 1
2010-04-01 12:00:31 2010-04-01 12:00:29

Standard operations on Dates are supported, including the difference, expressed in the unit of the left hand side Date in the operation:

from semperpy.core.date import *

a = Date(20090425)
b = Date(20090418)
print a - b
7
print b - a
-7

a = Date(2009042512)
b = Date(2009041800)
print a - b
180
print b - a
-180

a = Date(20090425)
b = Date(2009041812)
print a - b # days
7
print b - a
-156 # hours
print Day(b) - a
-7 # days

a = Date(2010030112)
b = Date(20090401)
print Month(a) - Month(b)
11 # months
print Year(a) - Year(b)
1 # year

Date objects are:

  • hashable

  • comparable: <, >, ==, <=, >=, !=

  • support basic arithmetics:

    • + (int | DateIncrement)
    • - (int | Date | DateIncrement)
    • += (int | DateIncrement)
    • -= (int | DateIncrement).
Date(date)¶
Parameters:date – another Date, a datetime or an integer (yyyy, yyyymm, yyyymmdd, yyyymmddhh etc..)

The Date class is a generic entry point into the Core.Date module, it returns an object allocated from another class, Year, Month, Day, Hour, Minute, Second.

Depending on the “accuracy” of the date, the right class will be allocated, either Year, Month, Day, Hour, Minute or Second. If a datetime is given, the class Second is used. You can specify which type you would like:

from datetime import datetime
from semperpy.core.date import *

d = datetime(2009,12,25,23,59)
myDateInDays = Day(d)
print d
2009-12-25

# these two statements are equivalent
d = Date(20090101)
d = Day(20090101)

# these two statements are equivalent
d = Date(200901011230)
d = Minute(200901011230)
datetime()¶

returns the Python datetime encapsulated.

year()¶

returns the year.

month()¶

returns the month.

day()¶

returns the day.

hour()¶

returns the hour.

minute()¶

returns the minute.

second()¶

returns the second.

intvalue()¶

returns the date as an integer or long integer in the form yyyy[mm[dd[hh[MM[ss]]]]].

format(format = None)¶
Parameters:format (string) – a datetime.strftime date format

returns a string formatted by datetime.strftime. The statement “print d.format()” is equivalent to “print d”.

The DateIncrement class¶

class semperpy.core.date.DateIncrement(object)¶

The DateIncrement is designed to give flexibility for various needs in date manipulation in different fields. DateIncrement makes it possible to increment a date by N months or N years or a combination of both. It is particularly useful for incrementing months:

d = Date(20050115)
inc = DateIncrement(months=3)
d += inc
print d
2005-04-15

DateIncrement is printable, can be negated and tested for equality against another DateIncrement.

DateIncrement(years=0, months=0, days=0, hours=0, minutes=0, seconds=0)¶

Creates a DateIncrement. All parameters are integers, these can either be positive of negative.

See also

Dates for examples.

class semperpy.core.date.Year¶

see Date.

class semperpy.core.date.Month¶

see Date.

class semperpy.core.date.Day¶

see Date.

class semperpy.core.date.Hour¶

see Date.

class semperpy.core.date.Minute¶

see Date.

class semperpy.core.date.Second¶

see Date.

The Dates function¶

semperpy.core.date.Dates(start1, end1, incr1, start2, end2, incr2, ..., startN, endN, incrN, format = None)¶

The Dates function (a sysnonym is DateSequence) generates a list of dates as integers from start1 to end1 with an increment of incr1 etc... if the parameter format is specified (as an strftime format), a list of string in that format is returned:

from semperpy.core.date import *

print Dates(20090115,20090415,DateIncrement(months=1))
[20090115, 20090215, 20090315, 20090415]

print Dates(20090115,20090415,DateIncrement(months=1),format="%Y-%m")
['2009-01', '2009-02', '2009-03', '2009-04']

print Dates(20090115,20090415,DateIncrement(months=1),20080115,20080415,DateIncrement(months=2))
[20090115, 20090215, 20090315, 20090415, 20080115, 20080315]

print Dates(20090115,20090430,DateIncrement(months=1,days=1))
[20090115, 20090216, 20090317, 20090418]

print Dates(2009011500,2009011612,12)
[2009011500, 2009011512, 2009011600, 2009011612]
Synonym :DateSequence
